pr_title: [WIP] Healthcheck support for specifying IP addresses as networks
pr_body: This implements some new functionality if the python 3 compatible ipaddress module is available (`py2-ipaddress`). ## Without `py2-ipaddress` Passing /32 IPs only: ``` # ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.4 --ip=1.2.3.5 --ip=1.2.3.6 --no-ip-setup INFO[healthcheck] send announces for UP state to ExaBGP announce route 1.2.3.4/32 next-hop self med 100 announce route 1.2.3.5/32 next-hop self med 101 announce route 1.2.3.6/32 next-hop self med 102 ``` Passing CIDR notation networks only: ``` # ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.0/30 --ip=1.2.3.4/30 --no-ip-setup usage: healthcheck.py [-h] [--debug | --silent | --syslog-facility [FACILITY] | --no-syslog] [--name NAME] [--config FILE] [--pid FILE] [--user USER] [--group GROUP] [--interval N] [--fast-interval N] [--timeout N] [--rise N] [--fall N] [--disable FILE] [--command CMD] [--next-hop IP] [--ip IP] [--no-ip-setup] [--label LABEL] [--start-ip N] [--up-metric M] [--down-metric M] [--disabled-metric M] [--increase M] [--community COMMUNITY] [--large-community LARGECOMMUNITY] [--as-path ASPATH] [--withdraw-on-down] [--execute CMD] [--up-execute CMD] [--down-execute CMD] [--disabled-execute CMD] healthcheck.py: error: argument --ip: invalid IPAddress value: '1.2.3.0/30' ``` _(note: same behaviour occurs with a mix of CIDR notation and IPs)_ ## With `py2-ipaddress` Passing /32 IPs only: ``` ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.4 --ip=1.2.3.5 --ip=1.2.3.6 --no-ip-setup INFO[healthcheck] send announces for UP state to ExaBGP announce route 1.2.3.4/32 next-hop self med 100 announce route 1.2.3.5/32 next-hop self med 101 announce route 1.2.3.6/32 next-hop self med 102 ``` Passing CIDR notation networks only: ``` # ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.0/30 --ip=1.2.3.4/30 --no-ip-setup INFO[healthcheck] send announces for UP state to ExaBGP announce route 1.2.3.0/30 next-hop self med 100 announce route 1.2.3.4/30 next-hop self med 101 ``` Passing mixed /32 IPs and CIDR notation networks: ``` ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.1 --ip=1.2.3.2 --ip=1.2.3.3 --ip=1.2.3.4/30 --ip=1.2.3.8/30 --no-ip-setup INFO[healthcheck] send announces for UP state to ExaBGP announce route 1.2.3.1/32 next-hop self med 100 announce route 1.2.3.2/32 next-hop self med 101 announce route 1.2.3.3/32 next-hop self med 102 announce route 1.2.3.4/30 next-hop self med 103 announce route 1.2.3.8/30 next-hop self med 104 ``` Passing mixed /32 IPs and CIDR notation networks, using the `--deaggregate-networks` option: ``` # ./healthcheck.py --ip=1.2.3.1 --ip=1.2.3.2 --ip=1.2.3.3 --ip=1.2.3.4/30 --ip=1.2.3.8/30 --no-ip-setup --deaggregate-networks INFO[healthcheck] send announces for UP state to ExaBGP announce route 1.2.3.1/32 next-hop self med 100 announce route 1.2.3.2/32 next-hop self med 101 announce route 1.2.3.3/32 next-hop self med 102 announce route 1.2.3.4/32 next-hop self med 103 announce route 1.2.3.5/32 next-hop self med 104 announce route 1.2.3.6/32 next-hop self med 105 announce route 1.2.3.7/32 next-hop self med 106 announce route 1.2.3.8/32 next-hop self med 107 announce route 1.2.3.9/32 next-hop self med 108 announce route 1.2.3.10/32 next-hop self med 109 announce route 1.2.3.11/32 next-hop self med 110 ``` Essentially this should make it easier to announce a block of IP addresses that fall within the same CIDR notation, and also advertise the whole CIDR block if not using `--deaggregate-networks`. One limitation is it's currently not possible to deaggregate some networks but not others. Couple of things I'm not completely happy with right now: - Use of `has_py3_ipaddress` to change the program flow - Command line argument usage, should this be a completely separate option, `--network`, available if the correct module is installed? - No tests & not tested on Python3 Thoughts on the implementation, suggestions for improvements etc? --- This change is [](https://reviewable.io/reviews/exa-networks/exabgp/526)

- unhide some implicit casts (in particular search weight which was casted as `int` and so loosing some precision)

pr_title: Fix infinite recursion when sublassing DjangoFilterConnectionField
pr_body:
Issue was caused by #224 
if a class is subclassing `DjangoFilterConnectionField` and a queryset is returned from `resolve_schema_field` the `super(cls,cls)` refers back to `DjangoFilterConnectionField` and has an infinite recursion.
I've subclassed `DjangoFilterConnectionField` to add orderby capabilities and it started erroring out because of infinite recursions
possibly a better fix would be
`queryset = DjangoFilterConnectionField.merge_querysets(default_queryset, queryset)`

pr_title: Bug 1486532 - consider task['tags']['worker-implementation'] when guessing worker implementation
pr_body:
In [bug 1486532](https://bugzil.la/1486532) empty properties in generic-worker task payloads in gecko decision task generated tasks will be disappearing. Currently, scriptworker guesses worker implementation based on properties that are included in the task payloads. Therefore, if these properties disappear, this guessing function will unfortunately no longer work. However, the good news is, there is trustworthy metadata contained in the gecko decision-task generated task definitions which provides this information, which is the `worker-implementation` tag. I suspect this didn't exist when the worker implementation guess code was written, but now that it does, it should be a reliable source of information.

I'm not sure if scriptworker evaluates tasks that are generated outside of the gecko decision task which may not have the `worker-implementation` tag, such as release tasks? Therefore, rather than assuming the presence of the `worker-implementation` tag, the previous mechanics of guessing the worker implementation have been left in place essentially as a fallback (with the added advantage that if they conflict with the tag, an exception will be raised).

Note, if the `worker-implementation` tag turns out to be present in all tasks that scriptworker evaluates, we could (and probably should) remove the former guessing code.

pr_title: Allow SNMP collector to address different communities
pr_body:
The SNMP collector uses the constant string 'agent' as the index for its CommunityData object.  This means multiple SNMP collectors will conflict, as second and subsequent collectors will use the same community name as the first collector, even if the diamond configuration specifies a different community.

Instead, include the community name in the index, so that each community has a unique index.

pr_body: These are the equivalente to [ac1fd72fe20b12 tests/unit/transfer/mediated_transfer/test_initiator.py](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ac1fd72fe20b12a97206eaf8529717b1e864b6bb/raiden/tests/unit/transfer/mediated_transfer/test_initiator.py) but with the channel state implementation.

This adds a `raiden smoketest` command. This fixes #679.

#### Rationale
The `smoketest` allows to check the "general sanity" of a raiden installation. Intended usages are
- fail early in CI (`raiden smoketest` will be run before the `pytest` sessions)
- allow to test the raiden CLI interface -- the raiden node started during the test uses the standard `raiden [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...` interface
- allow to test the functioning of `AppImage` builds
- help with debugging user issues

#### Operation
The `smoketest` starts a local private go-ethereum blockchain (with clique PoA) with a minimal set of raiden smart contracts already deployed. It will connect a raiden node and assert on the intended state of the raiden node after connection. So far the checks include:
- successful connection to the `Registry` contract
- successful connection to the `EndpointRegistry` contract
- successful update of the registered endpoint address for discovery
- successful collection and parsing of the (already setup) channel(s) from `Registry`

It generates a standardized report file after the test run.

#### Details
The configuration file, `smoketest_configuration.json`, is generated during the release build and shipped with the distribution. This is intended to achieve: a) allow users to run the command without the `solc` compiler and b) standardize the test results for a certain release.
However, if the installation uses a different `solc` version from the one recorded during building the distribution, the configuration will be updated and use the local `solc` compiler. The configuration that was used is included in the report file.

#### Changes tangential to the issue included
- removed redundant registry parsing from CLI (it is part of `RaidenService.__init__`)
- some refactoring to the test/fixture setup
- `raiden/tests` are now part of the distribution, so `pytest` and `psutil` became actual requirements 

#### Shortcomings
- I intended to make the ethereum client implementation customizable (see the comment block in `raiden/tests/utils/smoketest.py` prior to `DEFAULT_ETH_COMMAND`). This idea is not finished however, because the `init_with_genesis()` phase is not customizable yet. #758
- I intended to use a random free port for the ethereum rpc -- the current implementation will always use the same port. #759

    raise an exception, but will return False. Changed the test to check for

  'refersto:year:0-->9999').

  CFG_WEBSEARCH_MAX_RECORDS_CITEDBY.

  'InvenioWebSearchCitedbyLimitError'.

  is reached.

  'get_refersto_hitset', 'get_cited_by_list', 'get_citedby_hitset'

This PR adds a `@out.capture` decorator that pushes all the output of a function to an output widget. It addresses issue #1846 .

For instance, 

```py
out = widgets.Output()

@out.capture
def f(arg):
    print(arg)

f('argument')

out.outputs
# => ({'name': 'stdout',
  'output_type': 'stream',
  'text': 'argument'},)
```

This is useful for capturing output from widget callbacks:
```py
b = widgets.Button()
b.on_click(f)
# everytime someone clicks on `b`, `out.outputs` gets appended to.
```

To do:

 - [ ] Documentation on output widget (probably in a separate PR)
 - [ ] Use `functools.wraps` to avoid losing the function name and docstring (https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/functools.html#functools.wraps)
 - [ ] Is there a better name than `out.capture`?

pr_title: Attempt to prevent 'undefined' appearing in place of the hints button.
Add extra fields to a definition
pr_body:
Sometimes, I need to add a field to a definition.

Typical use case: the `discriminator` field (search for "discriminator" in [there](http://swagger.io/specification/#schemaObject), [this comment](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/403#issuecomment-128824351) also explains a lot and points some limitations). This can also be a vendor field (`x-my_field`).

I created a [specific definition helper](https://github.com/Nobatek/flask-rest-api/blob/master/flask_rest_api/spec/plugin.py#L111) for that, but I think it could be in apispec itself. There is nothing specific in it. So here's the PR.

I first added this to marshmallow plugin `schema_definition_helper`, but then I realized it was not specific to marshmallow schemas, so I moved it to `APISpec.definition`. I'm leaving the two commits on purpose to expose the two approaches. I can squash into one or remove the last one depending on which approach is preferred.

This feature is pretty permissive. A more conservative approach would be to allow only fields that conform to the spec: a finite hardcoded list _à la_ [`_VALID_PROPERTIES`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L172) + the possibility to pass any vendor field (`x-`). I'm not sure this is needed. After all, the user adds `extra_fields` explicitly, so he should know what he is doing. It's not like we're harvesting in some Schema's metadata. BTW, I don't think `discriminator` should be defined in a field's metadata. It is schema level stuff. It shouldn't be in `_VALID_PROPERTIES`, right?

I'd be happy to hear your feedback about this.

pr_body:
Added proposal as README file.

Please take a look @alope107

Huge refactoring to Livyclientlib to make some API's more sensible and the code simpler.  

Closes #185

* New `Command` class. This is a class which represents user code that should be executed against a session. Each `Command` has a GUID for use in triggering events.

* Remove `LivyClient` class (functionality subsumed by `Command`, `SqlQuery`, and `LivySession`)

* Rename classes to incorporate new changes (e.g. `ClientManager` -> `SessionManager`)

* Remove all references to "serialization" (we can add this back in when we decide on an HA story)

* Change API of `LivyReliableHttpClient`, which now has a few Livy-specific methods rather than generic `get` and `post` methods

* Change tests to accomodate these changes

* Change types of session ID string -> int

* Remove `LivySessionState` class (subsumed by `LivySession`)

* Change names of configurations to better reflect their meaning. Now there is a configuration `wait_for_idle_timeout_seconds` (which reflects the amount of time the library will wait to go to idle before executing a statement; defaults to 15 seconds) and `livy_session_startup_timeout_seconds` (which reflects the amount of time that the library will wait for the Livy session to start up).  

* Remove recursion in `wait_for_idle` and in `ReliableHttpClient` implementation

pr_title: Allow "name_or_alias" filtering operation on interface types.
pr_body: The compiler was unnecessarily strict with checking the validity of `name_or_alias` targets, and ended up disallowing its use on interface types that contained the necessary fields. This PR relaxes that restriction and updates the schema and tests to reflect that.

pr_title: Replace explicitly-tracked context data with QueryMetadataTable info.
pr_body:
Initial cleanup of the context data maintained by the compiler frontend, by replacing its data with QueryMetadataTable info. More cleanup coming, just trying to keep it in small pieces.

@jmeulemans this changes the `type` data in the QueryMetadataTable from a string type name to a GraphQL type object. This is to make it possible to use the QueryMetadataTable for the `location_types` values. I think it's not a big deal since it's easy to go from a type object to a type name, but it's much harder to go the other way.

Add test case as example of Git commit with invalid data
pr_body:
This is a real commit from the microjs.com open source project, see https://github.com/madrobby/microjs.com/commit/7e8457c17850d0991763941213dcb403d80f39f8, which is declared to be encoded in UTF-8, but contains invalid bytes.

This makes GitPython choke on it while decoding.  Rather than choking, this should instead accept the error and replace the invalid bytes by the � (\x80) char.
This fixes the documentation there: http://gitpython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference.html#git.repo.base.Repo.iter_commits

pr_title: Update Features to add more flexible Tooltip options with GeoJson.
pr_body:
Added a new Tooltip class to reference GeoJson feature properties, with tests, documentation, and examples. It's flexible, and can take field names, aliases, that can be turned on/off with the 'labels' boolean, a 'sticky' property, and can incorporate Javascript's .toLocaleString() functionality if desired. The same string can be passed for each object with the 'text' variable as well if you'd rather not use the 'fields'.

Also updated the GeoJson JS template to reference this Tooltip object, and added a test to make sure that the values passed exist in the properties.

pr_body:
## Motivation

Make writing roundtrip tests easier.   Fix #441 

## How to test the behavior?
```python
from . import base

# Roundtrip an NWBContainer i.e. something that isn't allowed in 'acquisition`
class TestElectrodeGroupIO(base.TestMapRoundTrip):

    def setUpContainer(self):
        self.dev1 = Device('dev1', 'a test source')  # noqa: F405
        return ElectrodeGroup('elec1', 'a test source',  # noqa: F405
                                        'a test ElectrodeGroup',
                                        'a nonexistent place',
                                        self.dev1)

    def addContainer(self, nwbfile):
        ''' Should take an NWBFile object and add the container to it '''
        nwbfile.add_device(self.dev1)
        nwbfile.add_electrode_group(self.container)

    def getContainer(self, nwbfile):
        ''' Should take an NWBFile object and return the Container'''
        return nwbfile.get_electrode_group(self.container.name)

# Roundtrip an NWBDataInterface i.e. something that can go in 'acquisition`
class TestUnitTimes(base.TestDataInterfaceIO):

    def setUpContainer(self):
        ut = UnitTimes('UnitTimes add_spike_times unit test')
        ut.add_spike_times(0, [0, 1, 2])
        ut.add_spike_times(1, [3, 4, 5])

    # roundtrip the container, and get it back for some additional tests
    def test_my_special_test(self)
        roundtripped = self.roundtripContainer()
        # now do something that doesn't test attribute equality

```
